description:
You are a data-engineering consultant who has to verify that a local mock “posts” API delivers
    the expected payload and then extract a small piece of data for use in a later ETL step.

    The mock API payload is already present on the filesystem and can be reached with a
    file-scheme URL:

        file:///home/user/mock_api/posts.json

    1. Use curl to download the JSON payload from the URL above and save it verbatim to
       /home/user/data/posts.json

    2. Extract every value that appears in a “title” field of that JSON file (there are exactly
       three of them).  Write those titles, one per line, **with no extra whitespace**, to

       /home/user/data/post_titles.txt

       Required order of the lines:
         • Build pipelines
         • Test ETL
         • Deploy to prod

    3. Create a simple two-line log file at /home/user/data/etl_curl.log with the exact format
       shown below (each line must be terminated by a single Unix newline character “\n” and
       nothing else):

           DOWNLOAD_OK posts.json
           EXTRACT_OK 3 titles

       • The literal strings DOWNLOAD_OK and EXTRACT_OK must be uppercase exactly as written.
       • posts.json and the number 3 must appear exactly as shown, separated from the status
         word by a single space.

    4. Make sure that all three files end up under the /home/user/data directory and that the
       permissions you leave allow the current (non-root) user to read them.

    The automated test will check:

       a. That /home/user/data/posts.json is byte-for-byte identical to what you downloaded.
       b. That /home/user/data/post_titles.txt contains exactly three lines, in the required
          order, each matching one title from the JSON.
       c. That /home/user/data/etl_curl.log contains exactly the two lines specified above and
          nothing more.

    You only need 2-3 shell commands to accomplish everything, but use whatever tooling you
    prefer (curl, grep, sed, awk, etc.).  Console output is allowed and encouraged so you can
    see what is happening while you work.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing mock API file that the agent will “download” -->
    <existing_file path="/home/user/mock_api/posts.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "posts": [
    { "id": 101, "title": "Build pipelines" },
    { "id": 102, "title": "Test ETL" },
    { "id": 103, "title": "Deploy to prod" }
  ]
}
]]></existing_file>

    <!-- Expected final state produced by the agent -->
    <expected_file path="/home/user/data/posts.json" must_match_existing="/home/user/mock_api/posts.json"/>
    <expected_file path="/home/user/data/post_titles.txt"><![CDATA[
Build pipelines
Test ETL
Deploy to prod
]]></expected_file>
    <expected_file path="/home/user/data/etl_curl.log"><![CDATA[
DOWNLOAD_OK posts.json
EXTRACT_OK 3 titles
]]></expected_file>
